_vars.raw_problem: Our company has recently launched a new product, and we want to assess its market performance. Please design a hypothesis test to determine if the average sales of this product exceed a certain benchmark that we have in mind. The test should evaluate the data collected from our sales team over the past three months. Additionally, prepare a report that discusses your method, findings, and any recommendations based on the results. Ensure to highlight any assumptions you make during your analysis and how they might affect your conclusions.
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Definition", "content": "The primary objective is to assess whether the average sales of the newly launched product exceed a predetermined benchmark. This involves formulating a clear null hypothesis (H0) and an alternative hypothesis (H1) based on the benchmark value. For instance, if the benchmark average sales are $X, then H0: μ ≤ X and H1: μ > X, where μ represents the true average sales of the product."}, {"section_type": "Data Collection", "content": "Data should be collected from the sales team's records over the past three months, ensuring that the dataset is representative and free from biases. This includes total sales figures, the number of units sold, and any relevant demographic information about the customers that could influence sales."}, {"section_type": "Statistical Methodology", "content": "To conduct the hypothesis test, I will utilize a one-sample t-test if the sample size is small (n < 30) and the population standard deviation is unknown, or a z-test if the sample size is large (n ≥ 30). The significance level (α) will be set at 0.05. It is crucial to check for normality in the sales data, as this will affect the choice of test. If the data is not normally distributed, a non-parametric test such as the Wilcoxon signed-rank test may be appropriate."}, {"section_type": "Assumptions", "content": "Key assumptions include: (1) the sales data is independent and identically distributed, (2) the sample size is sufficiently large for the Central Limit Theorem to apply if using a z-test, and (3) the sales figures are normally distributed if using a t-test. Any violations of these assumptions should be addressed, as they can lead to incorrect conclusions."}, {"section_type": "Analysis and Interpretation", "content": "After conducting the hypothesis test, I will calculate the p-value and compare it to the significance level. If the p-value is less than α, we will reject the null hypothesis, indicating that the average sales exceed the benchmark. The confidence interval for the mean sales will also be computed to provide a range of plausible values for the true average sales."}, {"section_type": "Reporting", "content": "The final report will include a detailed methodology section, results of the hypothesis test, interpretation of findings, and recommendations based on the analysis. It will also discuss the implications of the results for future marketing strategies and product adjustments, if necessary. Any limitations of the study and suggestions for further research will also be included."}]

criteria.name: Identification of Variables
criteria.description: Clearly identifies dependent and independent variables relevant to the hypothesis.
criteria.excellent_indicator: All relevant variables are identified with clear definitions and their roles in the hypothesis.
criteria.poor_indicator: Misses key variables or misidentifies their roles in the hypothesis.

criteria.name: Clarity of Hypothesis
criteria.description: Formulates a clear and testable hypothesis based on the problem statement.
criteria.excellent_indicator: The hypothesis is concise, specific, and directly addresses the problem.
criteria.poor_indicator: The hypothesis is vague, overly broad, or does not relate to the problem.

criteria.name: Contextual Understanding
criteria.description: Demonstrates a strong understanding of the context in which the hypothesis is framed.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Provides a thorough explanation of the context and its relevance to the hypothesis.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to explain the context or demonstrates a misunderstanding of its relevance.

typical_errors: Formulating a hypothesis that cannot be tested statistically.
Neglecting to provide context or background information for the hypothesis.

best_framing.base: Our company has recently launched a new product, and we want to assess its market performance. Please design a hypothesis test to determine if the average sales of this product exceed a certain benchmark that we have in mind. The test should evaluate the data collected from our sales team over the past three months. Additionally, prepare a report that discusses your method, findings, and any recommendations based on the results. Ensure to highlight any assumptions you make during your analysis and how they might affect your conclusions.

entries.title: Benchmark for Average Sales
entries.description: The specific benchmark for average sales that the company wants to test against needs to be explicitly defined. This will allow for a clear hypothesis formulation and statistical testing.
entries.addresses_gap: The specific benchmark for average sales is not provided.

entries.title: Sales Data Structure
entries.description: Details regarding the sales data structure, including sample size and distribution characteristics, are necessary to properly design the hypothesis test and ensure valid statistical inferences.
entries.addresses_gap: Details on the sales data structure (e.g., sample size, distribution) are missing.

entries.title: Significance Level
entries.description: The significance level (alpha) for the hypothesis test needs to be specified to determine the threshold for rejecting the null hypothesis, which is essential for the test's execution.
entries.addresses_gap: The significance level for the hypothesis test is not specified.

entries.title: Normality of Sales Data
entries.description: It is assumed that the sales data follows a normal distribution or that the sample size is large enough for the Central Limit Theorem to apply, which impacts the choice of statistical tests.
entries.addresses_gap: Assumptions about the distribution of the sales data need to be made for valid testing.

entries.title: Independence of Observations
entries.description: It is assumed that the sales data collected over the past three months are independent observations, which is crucial for the validity of the hypothesis test.
entries.addresses_gap: Assumptions regarding the independence of sales data need to be clarified.

entries.title: Statistical Significance
entries.description: The primary success metric will be whether the hypothesis test results in a statistically significant finding that the average sales exceed the benchmark. This will be determined by the p-value in relation to the significance level.
entries.addresses_gap: Success metrics for evaluating the hypothesis test results need to be defined.

entries.title: Confidence Intervals
entries.description: In addition to the hypothesis test, calculating confidence intervals for the average sales will provide insight into the range of values that likely contains the true average, which is a critical component of the analysis.
entries.addresses_gap: Additional metrics to evaluate the findings should be included.

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses the identification of variables by clarifying the benchmark and data structure, ensures clarity of hypothesis by specifying necessary details, and demonstrates contextual understanding by outlining assumptions and success metrics relevant to hypothesis testing.

criticism: The student's response effectively outlines the hypothesis and statistical methods but lacks clarity in identifying the specific benchmark value for average sales, which is crucial for hypothesis formulation. Additionally, while the student mentions the need for representative data, they do not explicitly define the dependent and independent variables, which are essential for understanding the hypothesis context.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would include a clear definition of the dependent variable (average sales) and the independent variable (time period or marketing efforts, if applicable). Additionally, explicitly stating the benchmark value for average sales would improve the clarity of the hypothesis. Providing a more detailed context about the product's market and potential factors affecting sales would enhance the contextual understanding.

per_criterion_scores.internal_note: The response implies but does not explicitly define the dependent and independent variables.
The hypothesis is clear but lacks specificity regarding the benchmark value.
The response demonstrates some understanding of the context but could benefit from more detailed background information.

strengths: Formulated a clear null and alternative hypothesis.
Outlined appropriate statistical methods for hypothesis testing.
refinements_correctly_selected: Assumptions about data distribution and independence
Use of confidence intervals as a success metric
refinements_missed: Explicit definition of benchmark for average sales
Identification of dependent and independent variables
